The corporate regulator has forced Viva Energy to book an extra $25 million in write-downs for the last financial year, embarrassing one of the country’s largest petrol station operators and its auditor, PwC.

The Australian Securities and Investments Commission found Viva, which [supplies about 25 per cent](https://www.afr.com/chanticleer/inside-albanese-s-plan-to-get-more-diesel-and-petrol-to-australia-20260409-p5zmky) of the country’s fuel by distributing Shell-branded petrol, oil and lubricants through a network of 1300 service stations, had incorrectly calculated impairment values of its retail outlets.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.378%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_1/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/02ce85ffbb639fa8c4aa6f10a94b530162fd91ac)

Viva Energy supplies about one-quarter of Australia’s fuel through its Shell service stations.  Jason Alden

A spokeswoman for PwC said the firm did not comment on client work.

Viva shares have climbed about 30 per cent in the past few weeks as fuel prices surged because of the fallout from the Middle East war, where Iran has been restricting vital oil shipments through the [Strait of Hormuz](https://www.afr.com/world/middle-east/trump-orders-naval-blockade-of-strait-of-hormuz-20260413-p5znb9).

Viva is the only manufacturer of aviation fuel in Australia and is a big supplier to the defence, aviation, transport and mining industries. Its refinery in Geelong, Victoria, was established in 1954.

Advertisement

Viva expanded its retail network two years ago with the [$1.2 billion acquisition of the On the Run business from the](https://www.afr.com/companies/retail/3-hot-dogs-and-a-mini-shop-behind-1-2b-petrol-bet-20241127-p5kttk) Shahin family, and is rebadging petrol stations and convenience stores to the OTR brand.

In a statement on Monday, the regulator said it had reviewed Viva’s financial reporting for the year to December 31, and decided the company had made an error in how it accounted for a $25 million impairment expense, out of total impairments for its retail sites of $559 million for the year.

Specifically, ASIC said it had concerns about the company’s approach to impairment testing of its convenience retail sites. That involved assessing some sites as a group, as part of the Shell Card division, rather than individually. The regulator said accounting standards required assets to be tested for impairment individually wherever possible.

A Viva spokesman said the company “appreciates the importance of robust financial reporting standards”, and said the change in approach “did not materially change the outcome and was made voluntarily”.

In August last year, Viva said a surge in illegal tobacco sales had forced it to write down the value of its retail sites, and blamed the illegal trade for a sharp decline in first-half profits.

Last week, Wynn Crawshaw released his thirtieth clothing collection.

It will be his last.

Amid a raft of challenges including a year of on-off Trump administration tariffs and softening spending, Crawshaw’s label, Wynn Hamlyn, will announce its closure this week. And the biggest blow for the 12-year-old business, Hamlyn says, has been the collapse of the brand’s most significant international wholesalers: Ssense and Neiman Marcus.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.092%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/339e1513c87437d0647812b0db152b69293df028)

Designer Wynn Crawshaw is closing his business after 12 years of trading. He points to fashion’s unsustainable wholesale model, and the closure of international retail partners, as the reason.  Adam Firth

“We are a seasonal business, so every season I think, ‘well, I’ll see what happens next season’,” says the 36-year-old, who lives in Auckland and travels regularly to Sydney and Melbourne, given [Australia is his brand’s largest market](https://www.afr.com/life-and-luxury/fashion-and-style/the-new-zealand-designer-ready-to-dress-australian-men-20250814-p5mmwr). “But it’s been hard. We could keep doing this forever because we love it, but it’s not sustainable.”

Crawshaw, who has shown at [Australian Fashion Week](https://www.afr.com/life-and-luxury/fashion-and-style/australian-fashion-week-has-a-new-familiar-home-20260219-p5o3oi) twice and whose clothes have been worn by the likes of Michelle Obama and Rita Ora, says the business had been profitable until “the past few years”.

Advertisement

Crawshaw says Neiman Marcus, whose parent, department store giant Saks Global, fell into bankruptcy in January, owes the business $US100,000 ($142,000). It’s one of a number of retailers that has struggled to pay its bills, he says. Aggressive discounting and returned inventory have cut into profit margins, too, and Crawshaw points the finger at the model itself.

“It’s not sustainable for a fashion company to have to wait 90 days after delivering a product – at a minimum – to be paid. I have my own suppliers to pay,” he says. Without working capital, Crawshaw has been unable to open a store in Australia, where he has been looking for a venue for more than a year. This has stunted the brand’s growth in the region, he says.

Wynn Hamlyn is sold at Incu, Mode Sportif and The Iconic. “Wholesale in Australia has been terrific. I am truly grateful to those retailers,” Crawshaw says, adding this “can’t offset what is happening globally”.

Vincent Wu, the co-founder of Incu, which specialises in high-end designer clothing and caters largely to younger, more creative customers, says Wynn Hamlyn has “performed well” for the store. “Our customers want local brands. Wynn was consistently a good seller for us,” Wu adds.

But it is a difficult time to be a smaller brand, Wu says.

“It takes a lot of time and resources to succeed overseas. It’s so competitive. It’s not just money, you have to spend time there and get to know the market.”

Advertisement

While there is potential to reach a big audience overseas, “it takes a significant amount of capital to make that work. If you’re Zimmermann you have the resources to be in Paris or New York all the time. But if you’re not at the forefront of customers’ and retailers’ minds, it’s very hard to grow.”

## Fashion industry struggles

Crawshaw’s brand is emblematic of broader shifts in the fashion landscape. Even at the pointy end, fashion sales are struggling.

This week, some of the world’s largest fashion businesses including LVMH, Kering and Prada will report their first quarter earnings for the year.

Most analysts predict softened results that reflect the sliding share prices of these groups and shaken consumer confidence. LVMH shares have slumped about 28 per cent in the first three months of the year, worse than during the 2009 financial crisis or the early days of pandemic in 2020.

At the other end of the spectrum, mass retailers such as Mosaic Brands – owner of Katies, Noni B and Rivers – cannot find a foothold and are closing. Local department stores have struggled, too. Myer’s profit slumped 30 per cent to $36.8 million last year while David Jones is [cutting staff to quell losses](https://www.afr.com/companies/retail/david-jones-squeezes-suppliers-cuts-staff-in-fight-for-survival-20260407-p5zlv4).

Advertisement

Other independent labels like Wynn Hamlyn are feeling the impact. In the past two years, Australian labels Dion Lee, Arnsdorf and Nique have shut. For them, the shuttering of big international partners such as Saks has been a particular problem. _The Australian Financial Review_ reported in January that Saks, which also owned its eponymous store and Bergdorf Goodman, owed money to [Australian brands such as Camilla, Aje and Rebecca Vallance](https://www.afr.com/companies/retail/from-aje-to-camilla-luxury-labels-owed-millions-after-saks-collapse-20260120-p5nvg9).

Despite those difficulties, Crawshaw has been trying to expand his brand in Australia, and had been in discussions with several investors to raise the money for a store in Sydney – the label has an outlet in New Zealand – and to increase production. While he says he was open to an investment “lifeline” for the business, earlier discussions had ultimately reached an impasse.

“They weren’t happy to raise capital based on our profitability,” he says.

Crawshaw says that if he had another year of funding, the business could have filled the gap left by the failure of its global wholesaling partners.

Without that, and plenty of uncertainty, he says it’s better to close.

“Even shipping this most recent collection from China to New Zealand cost us 50 per cent more than it did last season. We can’t keep going like this because the reality is, I don’t know how bad it will get,” Crawshaw adds.

A growing cohort of Australians who buy later in life and are facing rising interest rates and cost-of-living pressures will retire with outstanding housing debt, increasing the pressure on policymakers to reform the pension system and to increase housing supply.

Refinancing inquiries among those aged 55 and over surged 12 per cent year-on-year in February, the fastest growth in any cohort, according to credit bureau Equifax. Among borrowers aged 46 to 55, inquiries rose 8 per cent.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.092%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/61a281793029a3fc007699f53461be5aa2e3d364)

First home buyers Cameron and Chantelle Hill, pictured with their duck Peanut and dogs Loki and Lexi, feared being forced to rent for life so they made huge sacrifices to buy a house. Dan Peled

Australians are [paying off mortgages much later in life](https://www.afr.com/chanticleer/why-older-australians-are-becoming-a-housing-pain-point-20260320-p5r1z7), and an increasing number do not expect to retire mortgage-free. More than 40 per cent of those surveyed by Loan Market Group, a brokerage that claims a 25 per cent national market share, do not expect to have paid off their loans before retirement. Almost one-quarter do not even have a financial retirement strategy.

Australia’s aged pension system was designed with the idea that people would retire owning their home. The family home is not counted in means-testing for pension eligibility while financial assets are, and pension payments are too low to cover private rental market costs.

Boosting ownership, which has fallen from a peak of 71 per cent in 1966 to 67 per cent at the last census in 2021, is one [argument for reforms](https://www.afr.com/property/residential/why-falling-homeownership-will-change-how-australians-retire-20211117-p599nc) to curb tax breaks on housing investment.

Advertisement

Another is to reform pension eligibility, says Matthew Bowes, a senior associate in Grattan Institute’s Economic Prosperity and Democracy Program

“We’re going to see more renters retiring in future,” Bowes said. “At the moment, they are disadvantaged if they have savings outside the family compared with people having savings in the family home.”

The think tank advocates for the value of a family home above a threshold of $750,000 to be included in the pension means test, a measure it estimates could save the budget about $4 billion annually.

“We’re seeing increasing concern by Australians about intergenerational equity and this is one reform that could go towards alleviating that,” Bowes said.

Without major changes, the trend of older home buyers is likely to continue.

The average age of a first home buyer is 34, with one in five first home buyer loans issued last year going to people aged over 40, according to Westpac, suggesting that fewer Australians will be free of their 30-year housing debt at the end of their working life.

Advertisement

## ‘We were just desperate’

Chantelle Hill and her husband, Cameron, rushed in February to buy a three-bedroom house in the semi-rural suburb of Bellmere, on the outskirts of Brisbane’s north, for $825,000.

The couple, both 30, compromised on property size and location, moving more than an hour away from their family and friends, so they could get into the market before house prices rose above their borrowing capacity.

“We had to get into the market now or else we wouldn’t be getting into it. We were just desperate to find what we could get into. This isn’t really going to be our long-term house,” Chantelle Hill said.

“I just felt like, if we didn’t get in now, then we weren’t going to get in and we were just going to be left behind and be stuck renting for the rest of our lives.”

The national median dwelling value is $930,000, [according to Cotality](https://www.afr.com/property/residential/sydney-melbourne-home-prices-fall-with-affluent-suburbs-hit-hardest-20260326-p5ziu8). There are [no affordable entry-level houses](https://www.afr.com/property/residential/astounding-no-affordable-houses-for-first-home-buyers-in-any-city-20260223-p5o4qe) in any Australian capital city, the type suitable for first home buyers looking to start a family. [In Sydney, 55 per cent of suburbs](https://www.afr.com/property/residential/pretty-scary-data-points-show-why-young-parents-are-leaving-sydney-20260128-p5nxqm) have medians more than nine times higher than the median household income.

Advertisement

The generational divide is clear in the declining home ownership figures. Baby Boomers are three times more likely to have bought a house before turning 40 than Millennials, according to the latest Australian Bureau of Statistics data.

The decrease in home ownership and increase in Australians retiring with home loan debt will have knock-on effects for wealth planning and the superannuation system.

Hill said she and her husband had not thought about retirement when they bought their home.

“We just believe we’re still young, and that’s something for maybe 10 or so years down the track to think about,” she said.

Grattan Institute’s Bowes said people with high super balances could use some of that to pay off outstanding mortgage debt when they retired.

“As Australians retire with ever larger super balances, it’s appropriate that some of those savings are used to pay for housing,” he said.

Advertisement

But the big housing challenge that governments needed to tackle were ensuring there was greater choice of housing available for downsizers in cities, boosting rent assistance to support retirees who rent, and shifting away from stamp duty as a tax base, he said.

## The pitfalls of only relying on super

Wealth adviser Patricia Howard said getting into the property market was the most important step to having a financially secure retirement, even if it meant making compromises.

“It’s really important, even for people in their 40s, if they can take that first step on the property ladder, to do that,” said Howard, managing director at Howard Osmond Financial Services.

“The worst situation you could find yourself in is if you decide, ‘I’m never going to buy the house of my dreams, so I’m just going to ignore the property market and just going to let my super accumulate and hope that it will look after me’.”

The people likely to struggle most in retirement would be those receiving the full aged pension and Commonwealth Rent Assistance, she said.

“The rental supplement in this country is so insufficient that no age pensioner can survive on it, even living in a caravan park. It is just that bad.”

_San Francisco_ \| Meta is building an artificial intelligence version of Mark Zuckerberg that can engage with employees in his stead, as part of a broader push to remake the big tech company around AI.

The $US1.6 trillion group has been working on developing photorealistic, AI-powered 3D characters that users can interact with in real time, according to four people familiar with the matter.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.523%2C$multiply_2%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_145%2C$y_9/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/b0749ef1300b37fb0a049140a62ffa9421f71a6f)

Mark Zuckerberg has become increasingly hands-on as he oversees Meta’s AI push.  Bloomberg

The company recently began prioritising a Zuckerberg AI character, three of the people said.

The Meta chief is personally involved in training and testing his animated AI, which could offer conversation and feedback to employees, according to one person.

They added that the character is being trained on the billionaire’s mannerisms, tone and publicly available statements, as well as his own recent thinking on company strategies, so that employees might feel more connected to the founder through interactions with it.

Advertisement

The effort, which is at an early stage, is separate from Zuckerberg’s project to build a “CEO agent” to support him in his role, for example by retrieving information quickly. That idea was first reported by _The_ _Wall Street Journal._

Zuckerberg has gone on a [multibillion-dollar spending spree](https://www.afr.com/technology/coreweave-meta-strike-latest-30b-deal-for-ai-computing-20260410-p5zmq6) over the past year, promising to develop “personal superintelligence” and catch up with rivals such as OpenAI and Google in building cutting-edge models.

On Wednesday, Meta released [Muse Spark](https://www.afr.com/technology/zuckerberg-reveals-costly-ai-model-to-power-whatsapp-and-instagram-20260409-p5zmdl), a small, closed “purpose-built” model for use across its products, with advanced capabilities in areas such as health reasoning and visual understanding. Wall Street investors welcomed the release, with Meta’s shares rising 7 per cent on the day.

Zuckerberg has become increasingly hands-on as he oversees Meta’s AI push, according to people familiar with the matter. He has spent five to 10 hours a week coding on different AI projects at the company and sitting in on technical reviews, said one person.

In September 2023, Meta launched its Meta AI assistant as well as a range of AI-powered chatbots exhibiting different personalities based on celebrities such as Snoop Dogg, who agreed to have their voice and likeness used in the feature.

The so-called AI characters were developed as Zuckerberg noted the success of AI companion start-up Character AI, particularly with younger users, according to several people familiar with the matter.

Advertisement

Meta later rolled out an “AI Studio”, which allows users to generate their own AI characters, or creators to build AI versions of themselves to chat with fans.

However, the persona efforts faced controversy last year following reports that users were generating overtly sexual characters, amid concerns from the public and regulators over child safety. Since January, Meta has restricted teen access to its AI characters.

According to people familiar with the matter, Meta’s newly formed Superintelligence Labs have explored a fresh set of characters.

The company has focused in part on making photorealistic embodiments of virtual AI characters, four people said. But scaling the effort has been difficult as the technology requires lots of computing power to achieve realism and avoid a lag in interactions with users.

Meta has also been working on improving voice interactions with the characters. Last year, it acquired two voice companies, PlayAI and WaveForms.

The Zuckerberg character will be trained on images of the chief executive as well as his voice, one person said. If the experiment is a success, influencers and creators might one day be able to do the same, the person added.

Advertisement

Meta has been pushing employees to use AI technology internally to streamline processes and become more efficient. Employees are being encouraged to use agentic tools from the open-source software OpenClaw and design their own agents to automate tasks.

Product managers are being invited to do an AI-focused “skills baseline exercise”, according to several people familiar with the matter. This includes a technical system design test, as well as an exercise in “vibe coding”.

Some staff fear this could be a prelude to job cuts. Meta said the exercise is not mandatory and designed to establish where product managers might need extra training and development.

US President Donald Trump has declared on his social media website that Britain should “go get your own oil” from the Persian Gulf and sneered, “you’ll have to start learning to fight for yourself, the USA won’t be able to help you any more”. His defence secretary, Pete Hegseth, mocks “the big bad Royal Navy” too. To judge by this warm-up, the timing of King Charles III and Queen Camilla’s visit to Washington later this month is unpropitious.

Often, when the Anglo-American relationship is going through a sticky patch, His Majesty’s government likes to send the royals to smooth ruffled feathers – but usually not while [a quarrel is in full swing](https://www.afr.com/world/middle-east/charm-offensive-king-charles-stays-calm-carries-on-with-trump-visit-20260401-p5zkgj). Still, there are precedents for playing this gilt-edged diplomatic card.

news.description: Orams Group Trading Update. Orams Marine Services Limited, New Zealand's leading marine maintenance and refit services business,.
news.markdown:
13 April 2026

ASX Market Announcements
Australian Stock Exchange Limited

Australian Stock Exchange Limited

via ASX Online

MARKET UPDATE

Ariadne Australia Limited (“Ariadne” or “the Group”) provides an update on the Group’s financial
performance to 31 March 2026, including commentary on material movements since 31 December 2025.

Orams Marine Services Limited, New Zealand’s leading marine maintenance and refit services business,
has delivered a strong operating performance for the nine months ended 31 March 2026, with turnover
up 30% and EBITDA up 70% on the prior corresponding period (“pcp”). The forward order book
remains robust, with bookings extending into 2027 and beyond. Consolidated net profit after tax at the
Orams Group Limited level, including its property division, was NZ$6.4 million (pcp: NZ$1.6 million),
reflecting strong operating momentum and reduced interest expense following the introduction of a
partner in November 2024, together with a more favourable interest rate environment.

Foreign Currency Movements
As previously disclosed on 26 February 2026, the Group held gross assets of $198.9 million at 31

As previously disclosed on 26 February 2026, the Group held gross assets of $198.9 million at 31
December 2025, of which 51.7% were denominated in foreign currencies, primarily NZD and USD.

During the half‑year, both currencies weakened against the Australian dollar, resulting in unrealised
translational foreign exchange losses of approximately $5.1 million.

Since 31 December, both the NZD and USD have continued to weaken. Movements to 31 March 2026
have resulted in additional unrealised foreign exchange impacts reflected across both Profit or Loss and
Other Comprehensive Income (“OCI”), including an estimated $2.5 million movement in the foreign
currency translation reserve recognised in OCI primarily relating to the Group’s substantial investment
in Orams Group Limited. These translational impacts do not reflect changes in the underlying
performance or asset quality of the Group’s investments.

• Webjet declined from $0.88 to $0.53 per share, resulting in an approximate $6.9 million
valuation decrease, recognised through Profit or Loss.
• Hillgrove Resources declined from $0.046 to $0.036 per share, resulting in an approximate $2.0

• Hillgrove Resources declined from $0.046 to $0.036 per share, resulting in an approximate $2.0
million valuation decrease, recognised through OCI.
• Coast Entertainment Holdings declined from $0.550 to $0.475 per share, resulting in an

• Coast Entertainment Holdings declined from $0.550 to $0.475 per share, resulting in an
approximate $1.8 million valuation decrease, recognised through OCI.

* * *

ARIADNE

Cover Genius is progressing a fundraising round at a valuation materially above that underpinning the
Group’s current carrying value of its investment ($11.4 million)a(Subject to completion on the proposed
terms, this implies a potential uplift of approximately $4.5 million. Ariadne invested a total of $2.9 million
in Cover Genius across two tranches in 2018 and 2019 and has realised $1.5 million through partial sales
in 2022 and 2023. Following the fundraising, the implied value of the remaining holding would be $15.9
million, representing a total value realised and held of $17.4 million compared with $2.9 million invested,
equating to a 5.9x multiple on invested capital.

Financial Performance to 31 March 2026 vs 31 December 2025 (HY26)
The combined effect of the above movements, broader portfolio changes and administration costs

In March, Ariadne exited its investment in ClearView, at an uplift in valuation of $0.5 million from 31
December 2025, generating cash proceeds of $3.5 million.

The combined effect of the above movements, broader portfolio changes and administration costs
(excluding any potential uplift in Cover Genius) results in an estimated total comprehensive loss
attributable to members of approximately $5.2 million for the nine-month financial period to 31 March
2026 (HY26: $4.8 million net profit).

This estimated result for the nine-month financial period to 31 March 2026 comprises two elements:
• a net loss of $2.6 million (HY26: $2.8 million net profit) reported through Profit or Loss; and

• a net loss of $2.6 million (HY26: $2.8 million net profit) reported through Profit or Loss; and
• a net loss of $2.6 million (HY26: $2.0 million net profit) reported through OCI.

• a net loss of $2.6 million (HY26: $2.0 million net profit) reported through OCI.

These estimates are based on preliminary unaudited management accounts.

Ariadne continues to monitor foreign exchange volatility and its impact on the Group’s reported results.
These movements are non-cash and translational in nature, and do not reflect changes in the underlying
performance of the Group’s assets.

The Group remains well capitalised, with $23.3 million in cash at 31 March 2026, with a diversified
investment portfolio.

ENDS

Authorised for release by: The Board of Ariadne Australia Limited

Gary Weiss
Executive Director

For further information please contact:
Gary Weiss

Executive Director
+61 2 8227 5500

+61 2 8227 5500

news.description: On 9 April 2026, Sandon Capital Investments Ltd (SNC or the Company) announced a Share Purchase. Plan (SPP). Under the SPP, shareholders have the...
news.markdown:
Sandon Capital Investments Limited ACN 107 772 467 Level 11, 139 Macquarie Street, Sydney NSW 2000 13 April 2026 T: 02 8014 1188 F: 02 8084 9984 Dear Fellow Shareholder,

On 9 April 2026, Sandon Capital Investments Ltd (SNC or the Company) announced a Share Purchase Plan (SPP). Under the SPP, shareholders have the opportunity to subscribe for new SNC shares at a fixed price of $0.7625 per share, without brokerage. This price is equal to the pre-tax net tangible assets (NTA) per share as at 31 March 2026 ($0.7672), adjusted for the April 2026 monthly dividend ($0.0047), and represents a 4.1% discount to the closing market price on 8 April 2026.

The SPP allows Eligible Shareholders to add to their investment at the ex-dividend pre-tax NTA, which Directors consider to be a fair and equitable way to raise additional capital. Directors have capped the Offer at no more than 14 million new shares.

Some shareholders may reasonably question the timing of this offer. The conflict in the Persian Gulf continues to cause financial market and real-world volatility. These conditions are uncomfortable, and for many investors they make investment decisions harder, not easier.

That discomfort is often a feature of periods when opportunity may present itself. No one can know when confidence will return or geopolitical risks will subside, however we consider that markets tend to move ahead of improving economic conditions rather than waiting for them. Having additional capital available now is expected to give us greater flexibility to respond to opportunities sooner rather than later, when conditions may feel more comfortable but prices might be less attractive.

Our investment approach remains unchanged. We seek to invest in businesses with sound long-term prospects, where engagement is often required to realise that potential. As noted in recent monthly reports, several of our larger holdings have continued to report solid operating results despite weak share price performance. In our view, current prices in many cases reflect maximum pessimism rather than reasonable fundamentals.

Like many of you, I never seem to have as much cash available as I would like during periods such as this. Even so, I intend to invest through the SPP.

The SPP issue price implies an annualised fully franked dividend yield of 7.4% (or 9.9% grossed up for imputation credits). The new Shares will be entitled to receive the previously announced fully franked dividends for May and June 2026 provided they are owned on the respective record dates. Of course, future dividends beyond these are not guaranteed.

Payment in cleared funds for SPP Shares is due by 5.00pm (AEST time) on 24 April 2026. To access your personalised Application Form and to apply for SPP Shares, visit the SPP offer website at events.miraqle.com/SNC-spp

If you have any questions or would like to discuss SNC or its investments, please do get in touch.

## Thank you for your continued support.

Yours sincerely,

Gabriel Radzyminski Chairman, Sandon Capital Investments Ltd
